coredata.dc:title: Enhancing cementitious materials with polymer dots: Size effects on strength and chloride resistance
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, An-Nan
He, Chuang
Zheng, Shu-Yi
Guo, Hong-Ru
Long, Wu-Jian
coredata.dc:description: Polymer dots (PDs), as emerging carbon-based nanomaterials, show promise for improving cement material properties due to their small size, excellent dispersity, and affordability. However, the relevant research remains uncovered. Especially, the impact of PDs particle size on mechanical performance and chloride binding capacity is still blank. In light of this, this work offers a comprehensive insight into the size effects of PDs on the mechanical properties and chloride binding capacity of cement pastes, along with the corresponding influencing factors. Specifically, PDs with three particle sizes of ca. 13.69, 5.81, and 2.55 nm (namely, LPDs, MPDs, and SPDs) are efficiently synthesized by altering the reaction temperature based on the Schiff base reaction. In comparison with the blank group, the mechanical properties of paste specimens containing LPDs, MPDs, and SPDs are improved by 3.8 %, 12.9 %, and 16.5 % separately, and their chloride binding capacity are significantly enhanced by 53 %, 117 %, and 129 % respectively. More significantly, the relevant influence mechanism is that smaller-sized PDs can more effectively exert their nucleating effect to further facilitate the formation of C–S–H, further resulting in a denser pore structure and enhanced chloride physical adsorption in cement matrices. Meanwhile, the smaller-sized PDs are conducive to promoting Friedel's salt generation, dramatically improving the chloride chemical binding. This research would provide comprehensive guidance on synthesizing PDs suitable for cementitious materials to enhance their strength and anti-chloride-corrosion properties more efficiently, thereby fostering the development of high-performance cement composites.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer dots (PDs), as emerging carbon-based nanomaterials, show promise for improving cement material properties due to their small size, excellent dispersity, and affordability. However, the relevant research remains uncovered. Especially, the impact of PDs particle size on mechanical performance and chloride binding capacity is still blank. In light of this, this work offers a comprehensive insight into the size effects of PDs on the mechanical properties and chloride binding capacity of cement pastes, along with the corresponding influencing factors. Specifically, PDs with three particle sizes of ca. 13.69, 5.81, and 2.55 nm (namely, LPDs, MPDs, and SPDs) are efficiently synthesized by altering the reaction temperature based on the Schiff base reaction. In comparison with the blank group, the mechanical properties of paste specimens containing LPDs, MPDs, and SPDs are improved by 3.8 %, 12.9 %, and 16.5 % separately, and their chloride binding capacity are significantly enhanced by 53 %, 117 %, and 129 % respectively. More significantly, the relevant influence mechanism is that smaller-sized PDs can more effectively exert their nucleating effect to further facilitate the formation of C–S–H, further resulting in a denser pore structure and enhanced chloride physical adsorption in cement matrices. Meanwhile, the smaller-sized PDs are conducive to promoting Friedel's salt generation, dramatically improving the chloride chemical binding. This research would provide comprehensive guidance on synthesizing PDs suitable for cementitious materials to enhance their strength and anti-chloride-corrosion properties more efficiently, thereby fostering the development of high-performance cement composites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cementitious materials, as a ubiquitously utilized building materials for construction engineering, play a pivotal role in the advancement of society and the economy [1,2]. Nevertheless, in light of the increasing prevalence of high-performance, high-rise building structures, there is a growing necessity for the enhanced strength and durability of cement-based materials. Incorporating nanomaterials to improve the characteristics of cement-based materials is a praiseworthy strategy to fulfill this requirement. Specifically, nanomaterials can serve as nucleation sites, facilitating the production of a greater quantity of cement hydration products to refine the pore structure of cement matrices [3]. To date, nano-TiO2 [4], nano-SiO2 [5], layered double hydroxides (LDHs) [6,7], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [8], and graphene oxide (GO) [9–11] have demonstrated their exceptional effectiveness in improving the strength and durability of cementitious materials. However, on account of their substantial surface energy and specific surface area, the aforementioned nanomaterials are susceptible to serious aggregation within cement matrices, counteracting their enhancement effect and even detracting from the performance of cement-based materials (e.g. mechanical properties) [12,13]. Furthermore, the existing nanomaterials are subject to high costs, enormously constraining their engineering application [14]. These two knotty challenges considerably restrict the application of nanomaterials for the strength and durability improvement of cementitious materials.

Thankfully, polymer dots (PDs), as a newfangled carbon-based nanomaterial, possess outstanding dispersion and cost-efficiency, as well as boast facile preparation, appealing catalytic performance, and eco-friendliness [15,16]. Benefiting from these unique advantages, PDs have found widespread applications including detection [17], bio-imaging [18], catalysis [19], and optical device [20]. PDs are typically characterized as small, monodisperse carbon nanoparticles with unique fluorescent properties, consisting of highly cross-linked or slightly graphitized hydrophobic carbon core, hydrophilic polymer chain shell, and abundant surface groups [21–23]. These characteristics may confer excellent nucleating effect on PDs, enabling them to remarkably promote the mechanical performance and chloride binding ability of cement. More significantly, compared with existing nanomaterials, PDs are well-dispersed, sufficiently exerting their nucleating effect toward cementitious materials. This is anticipated to further amplify the properties of cement-based materials in terms of mechanics and chloride binding. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are no studies concerning the application of PDs in the field of cementitious materials so far. As a result, there is still a blank slate in the understanding of the effect of PDs on the properties of cement-based materials, necessitating urgent and comprehensive research efforts.

Previous researches demonstrate that the improved effectiveness of PDs is tightly related to their particle sizes. For instance, Sun et al. demonstrated that decreasing the size of PDs resulted in increasing both their fluorescence intensity and dispersion [24]. He et al. proposed that smaller-sized PDs exhibited improved capability to penetrate tissue and subcellular in the human body while also decreasing non-specific interactions [25]. Nevertheless, the size effects of PDs on the strength and durability of cementitious materials have yet to be revealed. It is essential to emphasize that the dimensions of nanomaterials play a paramount role in determining their influence on the strength and durability of cementitious materials. For instance, Chen et al. demonstrated that the smaller the nano-TiO2 size, the greater the compressive strength of nano-TiO2 blended cement mortars [26]. Inspired by this, Sun et al. evidenced that the cement pastes containing 5 nm nano-TiO2 displayed stronger compressive strength than that containing 25 nm nano-TiO2 at all curing periods. This was attributed to the fact that 5 nm nano-TiO2 was more effective in augmenting the hydration degree of cement, refining the pore structure, and densifying the cement paste [27]. Moreover, Qu et al. found that LDH with a smaller size (3.2 μm in diameter, 35 nm in width) significantly improved the compressive and flexural strength of cement mortar by 17 % and 55 %, and dramatically reduced their chloride migration coefficient by 53 %. This was ascribed to the higher specific surface area of the smaller-sized LDH, contributing to enhancing the mechanical and chloride binding properties of cementitious materials [28]. Based on the existing studies above, it is reasonable to deduce that the strength and durability of cementitious materials is inextricably linked to the particle size of PDs. Nontheless, there is still a critical knowledge gap regarding the size effects of PDs on the mechanical properties of cement pastes, as well as their chloride binding capacity. More importantly, the underlying influence mechanisms remain undisclosed. Therefore, it is imperative to synthesize diverse sizes of PDs and comprehensively explore their size effects on both the mechanical performance and chloride binding capacity of cementitious materials. This will contribute to the improvement of the mechanical properties and chloride binding ability of cement-based materials by preparing PDs with optimal sizes.

In light of this, this study controllably prepares PDs with three distinct sizes, systematically examines their impact on the mechanical performance and chloride binding capacity of cement pastes, and thoroughly uncovers the underlying mechanisms. Specifically, PDs with three particle sizes are effectively fabricated based on the Schiff base reaction using 1,2-Phenylenediamine (o-PD) and 1,4-Benzoquinone (p-BQ) as precursors, and their particle size is controlled by simply adjusting the reaction temperature. The optical images, Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis), and photoluminescence (PL) spectrums certify their preferable long-term stability in saturated Ca(OH)2 (CH) solution (i.e., simulated concrete pore solution (SCPs)). The mechanical capacity and chloride binding property of cement pastes containing various PDs are methodically determined by the compressive test and the modified chloride adsorption equilibrium method, respectively. The underlying mechanism is logically presented through the phase compositions and microstructure analyses from X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), and Mercury Intrusion Porosimetry (MIP). This work would provide detailed guidance for the synthesis of novel PDs additive with ideal diameter sizes, ultimately aiming to promote the advancement of nano-modified high-performance cement composites.

The reagents for synthesizing PDs including o-PD and p-BQ were purchased from Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin China). All chemicals utilized in the experiment were of analytical reagent (AR) grade and did not necessitate further purification prior to their application. The experimental procedures exclusively employed deionized water, while the cement employed in this work was PI 52.5R Portland cement (PC). Tables 1 and 2
outlined the distinct features and makeup of PC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PDs with different sizes
ce:section.ce:para: Three different sizes of PDs were synthesized by a Schiff base reaction utilizing o-PD and p-BQ as precursors, with size being controlled by changing the reaction temperature. The detailed synthesis process was depicted in Fig. 1 , o-PD and p-BQ were combined in a 1:1 mass ratio in deionized water to create a homogeneous mixture, aided by ultrasonic agitation for 5 min. Afterward, the blend was consistently stirred for a duration of 3 h at 25, 50, and 70 °C individually using a temperature-controlled incubator shaker. The resulting solution, which was dark brown in color, underwent purification by centrifugation and vacuum filtration with a 0.2 μm membrane. This was followed by dialysis using a 100–500 Da membrane to remove any remaining particles and unreacted materials. Finally, solid brown PDs were obtained after undergoing rota-evaporation and vacuum freeze drying and subsequently defined as LPDs, MPDs, and SPDs respectively according to their reacting temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mixing proportions and fabrication process of specimens
ce:section.ce:para: Four different types of cement paste specimens without/with varying sizes of PDs were fabricated to investigate the size effects of PDs on the mechanical properties and chloride binding behavior of cement pastes. Based on our previous research [29–31], it was found that the most effective dosage of other carbon-based nanomaterials to improve the mechanical and chloride binding properties of cement composites was 0.2 wt% (by mass of cement). Therefore, the dosage of PDs with varying sizes was also set at 0.2 wt% in this work. Furthermore, the water-cement ratio (w/c) employed for chloride binding analysis was set at 0.5, as indicated in previous studies [32,33]. However, considering that PDs at a w/c of 0.5 significantly enhanced the fluidity of cement pastes while adversely affecting their mechanical properties, all specimens exhibited a w/c of 0.42 for mechanical testing. Three different sizes of PDs were dissolved in deionized water while preparing paste specimens. The resulting solution was then incorporated into cement, followed by thorough mixing using a mechanical stirrer. The cement mixture was placed into a plastic container with dimensions of 40 mm × 40 mm × 40 mm and then sealed with plastic wrap to prevent moisture from escaping. After the cement pastes had solidified, the samples were taken out of the molds and placed in a curing chamber at a temperature of 20 ± 2 °C and a relative humidity of over 95 %.

The morphology of PDs was investigated employing transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images obtained from FEI Talos F200S and Brucker MFP-3D infinity separately. Fourier infrared (FTIR, Thermo Scientific IS10), Raman (Thermo Scientific DXR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250) spectra were used to characterize the structure information and chemical composition of PDs. Additionally, UV–vis and PL spectra obtained by PerkinElmer Lambda 750 instrument and Hitachi F7000 separately were used to investigate the dispersion stability of PDs in SCP solution.

Compressive strength tests were conducted on paste samples following a 7- and 28-day curing period regarding the Chinese national standard GB/T 17671-2021. After curing for 7 and 28 days, the paste samples were immersed in dry ethanol for 3 d to stop hydration, then dried in an oven at 60 °C until fully dried. The compressive strength was tested by a YAW-300B full-automatic pressure tester. The loading rates were 2400 ± 200 N/s. To reduce inaccuracies, three measurements were taken for each variety of paste specimens.

The chloride binding performance of various paste specimens was assessed using the chloride adsorption equilibrium method described in previous literature [34]. It should be noted that certain experimental steps were modified, which might prevent the samples from fully reaching adsorption equilibrium after 14 d. However, this modification did not compromise the validity of the conclusions, as the trends in chloride binding capacity relative to the particle size of PDs remained observable. The procedure was as follows: following a 28-day curing period, the specimens were placed in anhydrous ethanol for 3 days to stop hydration, then dried in an oven at 60 °C until completely dry. The dried specimens were subsequently ground into a fine powder with particle sizes not exceeding 0.70 μm. Each specimen was allocated 4 g of the cement powder and placed in a 120 mL centrifuge tube containing 40 mL of a 3 mol/L NaCl solution. Each centrifuge tube was securely sealed and shielded from exposure to sunlight and fluctuations in temperature to prevent the crystallization of chloride salts resulting from evaporation, and frequent agitation of the tubes was employed to facilitate thorough interaction between the cement powder and the chloride. After being centrifuged at different periods (1, 7, 14, and 28 d), the supernatant was separated and used to determine the concentration of chloride ions. Free chloride ion concentration was determined using an automated potentiometric titrator with a 0.1 mol/L AgNO3 solution as the titrant. The chloride content bound within each specimen was computed according to Equation (1).

signified the molar mass of water at 18 g/mol. It should be noted that other chloride-containing phases Kuzel's salt (Ks) exist solely at chloride concentrations below 0.5 mol/L [36]. Given that the NaCl solution used in this study has a chloride concentration of 3 mol/L, the total chemically bound chloride is associated exclusively with Fs and does not account for Ks. Furthermore, it should be clarified that Fs may also form solid solutions with other phases (such as monocarbonate or hemicarbonate). In Equation (3) the assumption of a Cl/Al ratio of 1 in Fs was adopted to simplify the calculations, without considering other chloride-containing solid solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase compositions and microstructure of various samples
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the sizes effects of PDs on the mechanical properties and chloride binding of cement pastes, the phase compositions and microstructures of different cement pastes after 28-day curing and an additional 28-day immersion were examined using XRD, DTG, SEM, and MIP. To acquire the testing samples, the powder specimens derived from the compressive strength test were pulverized into fine powder. The paste powder used for the chloride binding test was obtained by extracting it from a mixture of NaCl solution and paste specimens. Afterward, the sample was washed two times with deionized water to remove any remaining NaCl solution, followed by vacuum drying at 60 °C until a constant weight was reached.

XRD spectra were collected using a D8 Advance diffractometer manufactured by Brucker, equipped with a copper target for X-ray generation. The diffractometer functioned at a scan rate of 0.2°/s, utilizing a voltage of 40 kV and a current of 40 mA. For quantitative analysis of the XRD data utilizing the Rietveld refinement method, each sample was blended with 20 wt% ZnO and homogenized in an agate mortar. During semi-quantitative analysis using the Rietveld refinement method, each result was verified to have a goodness of fit value below 2.

The Differential thermogravimetric (DTG) analyses were conducted using the STA-409PC instrument manufactured by Netzsch. Each test included the analysis of around 50 mg of powder specimen in a nitrogen environment, with a gas flow speed of 10 °C/min, and a specified temperature range of 30–1000 °C.

SEM imaging was used to examine the microstructural characteristics of the samples with the Thermo APREO S instrument from Thermo Scientific. An SEM image was taken at either 10,000 × or 20,000 × magnification, utilizing secondary electron mode with a voltage of 5 kV and a 10 mm working distance.

Porosity in the paste samples was evaluated using MIP analysis with the AutoPore IV 9500 Micromeritic instrument. For the experiment, specimens with varying thicknesses between 2 and 6 mm were employed, and mercury (Hg) with a specific gravity of 13.5335 g/mL was injected into the empty spaces under pressures ranging from 0.1 to 60,000 psi. The contact angle was established at 130°.

In this study, three PDs with varying particle sizes are prepared by only adjusting the reaction temperature. Following this, a thorough characterization is performed to uncover the morphology, structural properties, and chemical composition of the as-synthesized PDs.

The morphologies of diverse PDs are assessed using TEM and AFM. Fig. 2 illustrates their TEM images and size distributions. Notably, all the PDs are quasi-spherical and mono-dispersed without any agglomeration (Fig. 2a–c). By stochastically counting over 130 particles, Fig. 2d–f manifests their particle size distributions. Precisely, LPDs synthesized at 25 °C display a particle size distribution of 12.0–15.5 nm, with an approximate average size of 13.69 nm. MPDs exhibit a size distribution of 4.0–7.5 nm and an average size of 5.81 nm as the reaction temperature is 50 °C. Elevating the reaction temperature to 70 °C results in a decrease in the size range of the SPDs from 1.0 to 4.5 nm, with an average size of around 2.55 nm.

AFM images (Fig. 3 ) provide information on the topographical height of different PDs. In detail, Fig. 3a–c shows that all the PDs are well-dispersed, corresponding to the finding of TEM. Regarding the topographical height distributions of diverse PDs (Fig. 3d–f), LPDs prepared at 25 °C are primarily found within the range of 11.8–12.4 nm. As the reaction temperature extends to 50 °C, the height distribution of MPDs ranges from 4.4 to 6.3 nm, significantly lower than that of LPDs. As the reaction temperature elevates up to 70 °C, the topographical height of SPDs is minimal, displaying 1.6–2.2 nm. To sum up, TEM and AFM results validate that PDs possess outstanding dispersity, with their dimension decreasing as the reaction temperature rises from 25 to 70 °C.

Raman, FTIR, and XPS measurements are utilized to unveil the structural characteristics, functional groups, and chemical compositions of PDs with diverse sizes. As depicted in the Raman spectra (Fig. 4 a), all the PDs display two identical bands: the D band at 1341 cm−1 representing disordered sp3 defects, and the G band at 1589 cm−1 associating with sp2 carbon. The intensity ratios of two peaks (I D/I G) from LPDs, MPDs, and SPDs are greater than 1, specifically 1.17, 1.12, and 1.08, respectively. This suggests that all as-fabricated PDs exhibit a defective crystalline structure [37]. And the crystallinity of PDs exhibits a gradual increase as their particle size declines (i.e., the reaction temperature increases). With respect to the FTIR spectra (Fig. 4b), all the PDs show similar adsorption peaks, suggesting that the reaction temperature does not alter the functional groups on the surface of PDs. Specifically, a slight peak at 1627 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of C=O bond [38]. And the obvious peak observed at 1589 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibration of the C=N bond [39,40], demonstrating that the formation of PDs includes the Schiff base reaction. Moreover, the peaks observed at 3100-3600 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H bonds [41]; While the peaks at 1479, 1367, 1226, and 1103 cm−1 signify the existence of C–N, –NO2, N–H, and C–O–C bonds, respectively [40]. These numerous oxygen-/nitrogen-containing groups confer excellent dispersity on PDs within aqueous environments. XPS results offer an in-depth analysis of the chemical composition and chemical bonds of PDs. As depicted in Fig. 4c, all of the PDs exhibit consistent elemental compositions of C, N, and O in the full-range XPS spectrums. Further, the high-resolution C1s spectra manifest four distinct peaks (Fig. 4d): C–C/C=C, C–N, C–OH, and C=N/C=O. The N1s spectra (Fig. 4e) exhibit three discernible peaks related to C=N, N–H, and –NO2. In the O1s spectra (Fig. 4f), two distinct peaks are ascribed to C=O and C–O, respectively [40]. Table 3 indicates that three categories of PDs possess comparable percentages of C, N, and O elements. This suggests that alterations in reaction temperature only significantly affect the dimensions of PDs, yet exert a negligible effect on their chemical composition. Notably, LPDs possess the lowest ratio of C–C/C=C bonds (25.78 %), followed by MPDs (47.42 %) and LPDs (49.25 %). This indicates that as the reaction temperature elevates, the crystallinity of PDs progressively increases, aligning well with the observations of the Raman spectra (Fig. 4a). In conclusion, PDs with three distinct sizes are prepared via controllably adjusting the reaction temperatures. These as-synthesized PDs share notable differences in particle sizes, akin chemical compositions, and similiar surface functional groups. These insights are instrumental for investigating the influence of size effects on the properties of PDs-containing cement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion stability of different sizes of PDs
ce:section.ce:para: The dispersibility of nanomaterials is notoriously considered to be crucial in improving the performance of cement composites [42,43]. Consequently, benefiting from the distinctive optical performance of PDs, UV–vis and PL spectrums are utilized to substantiate the disperse stability of PDs with different sizes. As shown in Fig. 5 a, after 3-day standing, no discernible precipitation is observed in all the PDs-containing SCPs. Furthermore, when exposed to 365 nm UV light, all the PDs-incorporated SCPs exhibit a vivid orange fluorescence. This qualitatively confirms the superior dispersion stability of PDs within SCPs since the agglomeration of PDs attenuates or completely quenches the fluorescence (i.e. self-quenching effect) [44]. Moreover, the UV–vis (Fig. 5a) and PL (Fig. 5b) spectrums of all the PDs-containing SCPs exhibit nearly consistent peak intensities following 3-day standing. This quantitatively suggests that all the PDs with different sizes exhibit favorable stability over extended periods within simulated concrete environments. In brief, the results above suggest that PDs with different sizes consistently showcase phenomenal long-term dispersion stability, laying a strong cornerstone for their application in cementitious materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of diverse PDs-containing cement pastes
ce:section.ce:para: The size effects of PDs on the mechanical characteristics of cement pastes are investigated by analyzing the compressive strengths of specimens containing 0.2 wt% LPDs, MPDs, and SPDs following 7- and 28-day curing. As illustrated in Fig. 6 , all paste samples consistently display an increase in compressive strengths as the curing period prolongs. At the same time, three types of PDs with varying sizes continuously enhance the compressive strength of pastes irrespective of curing time. More importantly, as the sizes of PDs reduce from 13.69 to 2.55 nm, the compressive strength of paste specimens gradually amplifies at all the curing ages. In particular, after 28-day curing, the SPDs-C group containing SPDs measuring 2.55 nm manifests the greatest compressive strength of 47.43 MPa, followed by the MPDs-C group incorporating MPDs measuring 5.81 nm (45.96 MPa), and the LPDs-C group comprising LPDs measuring 13.69 nm (42.26 MPa), occasioning 16.5 %, 12.9 %, and 3.8 % higher than the blank group (40.70 MPa) respectively. This validates the efficacy of PDs with three particle sizes in improving the mechanical characteristics of cement pastes. The mechanical properties of cement pastes increase as the sizes of PDs decrease (ranging from 13.69 to 2.55 nm). And cement pastes experience the best modification effect on their mechanical behaviors when modified by SPDs measuring approximately 2.55 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chloride binding performance of diverse PDs-containing cement pastes
ce:section.ce:para: The modified chloride adsorption equilibrium test is applied to elucidate the size effects of PDs on the chloride binding capacity of cementitious materials. Fig. 7 a presents the bound chloride (C b) values in diverse paste specimens at varying immersion periods in NaCl solution. It should be highlighted that chloride equilibrium is typically reached within 10–14 d [34]. However, in Fig. 7a, the C b values for all paste specimens continue to increase even at 28 d. This is likely due to the modification of certain experimental parameters in our procedure, especially the concentration of NaCl solution and cement particle size. These modifications may result in the non-equilibrium state of paste samples. However, despite the paste specimens not reaching adsorption equilibrium, it does not affect the observed relationship between the size of PDs and the chloride binding capacity of cement pastes. Specifically in Fig. 7a, the C b values of all the specimens remarkably enhance as the immersion period extends. Meanwhile, the chloride binding capability of PDs-containing paste specimens apparently augments compared to the blank group regardless of immersion periods. And as PDs sizes reduce, the chloride binding behavior of PDs-incorporated paste specimens progressively enhances. Specifically, the SPDs-C group containing PDs with the smallest particle size demonstrates the greatest C b value of 54.06 mg/g after 28-day immersion, followed by the MPDs-C group (51.25 mg/g) and the LPDs-C group (36.03 mg/g), augmenting by 129 %, 117 %, and 53 % in comparison with the blank group. In addition, the alteration in particle sizes of PDs results in a notable impact on the increase rates of C b values (Fig. 7b). On the one hand, at 1-day immersion time, all the specimens share the greatest increase rate of C b values. And the increase rate progressively declines as the immersion period progresses, signifying that PDs elevate the chloride binding of cement pastes more tremendously at the early immersion stage. On the other hand, the increase rate of C b values gradually rises as PDs sizes decrease regardless of the immersion period, suggesting that the smaller-sized PDs endow cement pastes with better chloride binding capacity. In a word, these aforementioned observations validate that all the PDs with diverse sizes notably amplify the chloride binding ability of paste specimens, and PDs with smaller sizes demonstrate a stronger improvement. In other words, the facilitation of chloride binding induced by PDs is in negative proportion to their particle sizes between 2.55 and 13.69 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compositions and microstructure of different PDs-incorporated paste specimens
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the size effects of PDs on the mechanics and chloride binding abilities of cement pastes, a comprehensive analysis of the phase composition and microstructure of diverse paste specimens is conducted by adopting XRD, DTG, SEM, and MIP techniques.

XRD analysis (Fig. 8 a and c) is adopted to reveal the phase compositions of different paste specimens after 28-day curing and additional 28-day immersion, coupled with Rietveld refinement (Fig. 8b and d) for semi-quantitative analysis. It should be noted that all specimens are inevitably affected by carbonation and do not fully reflect the actual phase content. Therefore, the actual amount of hydration products (such as CH) is likely greater than the obtained results. However, since all specimens undergo the same treatment and preservation processes, the carbonation will not influence the observed trend. After 28-day curing (Fig. 8a), all the paste specimens present consistent characteristic peaks. Specifically, the peaks at 18.1°, 34°, and 47.1° are attributed to CH [31]. The peak at 29.4° corresponds to CaCO3 (Cc) [29], which can be ascribed to insufficient protection against carbonation during sample treatment and storage, giving rise to the reaction of hydration products (primarily CH) with atmospheric CO2 to generate Cc. Furthermore, the peaks at 32.3°, and 32.7° correspond to the unhydrated clinker C3S and C2S, respectively [45]. The above findings substantiate that the incorporation of PDs cannot alter the category of hydration products. As disclosed in Fig. 8b, all the PDs-doped paste specimens possess higher C-S-H content compared to the blank group (60 %), and the percentage of C–S–H in PDs-containing cement pastes elevates from 61 % to 64 % with PDs sizes reducing from 13.69 to 2.55 nm. Specifically, the SPDs-C group containing the smallest-sized PDs presents the maximum C–S–H percentage (64 %), followed by the MPDs-C group (62 %), and the LPDs-C group (61 %). Correspondingly, as the sizes of PDs decrease, the C3S/C2S contents also decline from 10 %/8 % in the blank group to 8 %/5 % in the SPDs-C group. This reflects that PDs can boost cement hydration, with smaller-sized PDs leading to stronger hydration promotion. Since C–S–H plays an irreplaceable role in improving the strength of cement-based materials and is primarily responsible for their chloride physical adsorption. Consequently, the inclusion of PDs amplifies the mechanical strength and chloride physical adsorption of cement pastes. And the smaller the PDs dimensions, the higher the strength of these properties. Following a 28-day submersion in NaCl solution (Fig. 8c), the paste specimens illustrate the emergence of newfangled peaks at 11.7°, 22.5°, and 23.7° attributed to Fs [30]. This confirms the occurrence of chemical binding resulting from the interaction between free chloride ions and AFm phases. It is noteworthy that the peak intensity at 18.1° of CH enormously mitigates following exposure to NaCl solution, probably due to the involvement of CH in the generation of Fs [46,47]. In Fig. 8 d, the percentages of C3S/C2S progressively diminish in all specimens, demonstrating that they are continuously depleted through further hydration while immersed in NaCl solution. More importantly, as the sizes of PDs become smaller, the contents of C3S and C2S reduce; While the content of C–S–H progressively elevates from 62 % in the blank group to 72 % in the SPDs-C group. This corroborates that PDs are able to further facilitate C–S–H formation after 28-day immersion, thereby augmenting the physical adsorption of chloride. Meanwhile, the chloride physical adsorption improves as the sizes of PDs decline. As for Fs, the addition of PDs additionally enhances its formation, and the content of Fs exhibits a constant increase as their sizes reduce. More precisely, the content of Fs escalates from 8 % in the blank group to 12 % in the SPDs-C group. It implies that PDs are effective in fostering the production of Fs, thhereby improving the chloride chemical binding of cementitious materials. Concurrently, the efficiency of chloride chemical binding improves as the dimension of PDs reduces from 13.69 to 2.55 nm.

DTG (Fig. 9) test is implemented to acquire more detailed insights into the hydration product contents of diverse paste specimens. As presented in Fig. 9a, all the specimens demonstrate similar weight loss peaks within specific temperature intervals, specifically at 50–150 °C (C–S–H/Ettringite (AFt)), 150–210 °C (AFm phases), 410–500 °C (CH), and 600–800 °C (Cc) [48–50]. This highlights that PDs do not alter the phase composition of hydrates, in conformity with the finding of XRD analysis. Given that prior literature has substantiated that C–S–H constitutes a majority of the content in cement hydration products, considerably more than AFt [2]. Hence, the alterations in the peak at 50–150 °C can be roughly interpreted as variations in the quality of C–S–H. Evidently, the weight loss peak of C–S–H from PDs-doped specimens is relatively greater compared to the blank group, and smaller-sized PDs bring about more C–S–H quantity. In particular, the SPDs-C group with the smallest-sized PDs manifests the most pronounced C–S–H weight loss peak. This fully proves that smaller-sized PDs are crucial in facilitating cement hydration, leading to an increased C-S-H formation, thus dramatically augmenting the mechanical characteristics and the chloride physical adsorption of cementitious materials. This is probably due to the improved nucleating effect of smaller-sized PDs, which provide more nucleation sites for hydration products such as C–S–H. After being submerged in a NaCl solution for 28 d (Fig. 9b), a novel weight loss hump belonging to Fs occurs within 260–360 °C in all the specimens [35], along with a marked enhancement in the weight loss peak of AFm phases. This implies that AFm phases are further formed following immersion; Concurrently, the chemical binding of chloride happens since Fs are generated. Moreover, the weight loss humps of AFm phases and Fs within PDs-incorporated paste specimens are tremendously stronger compared to the blank group, and their qualities gradually amplify as the size of PDs diminishes. In detail, the SPDs-C group shares the maximum content of Fs and AFm phases. This signifies that PDs promote the more generation of Fs and other AFm phases to increase the chloride chemical binding, with the extent of enhancement being directly related to the decrease in PDs sizes, coincidental with the deduction from XRD. Concerning C–S–H, its content within PDs-containing paste specimens escalates as the particle sizes of PDs dwindle. Among them, the SPDs-C group containing PDs with the smallest sizes exhibits the maximum amount of C-S-H. This is strongly evident that the inclusion of smaller-sized PDs further promotes the formation of more C–S–H, leading to better chloride physical absorption and greater mechanical properties in cement composites.

The chemically bound water (W b) content reflects the hydration degree of cementitious material. A higher W b value reflects more hydration products within cement pastes. In this work, the W b value for various paste specimens is determined using Equation (5) based on the TGA results. This calculation involves the mass loss observed between 50 and 550 °C [51], where M 50 and M 550 represent the mass of the sample after ignition at 50 °C and 550 °C, respectively. Given that the specimens experienced carbonation, the W b value is adjusted by calculating the decrease in W b in CH due to carbonation, utilizing the method outlined in Ref. [52]. The results are depicted in Fig. 10 a. Evidently, all PDs-containing groups exhibit higher W b values compared to the control group, suggesting that PDs boost the formation of hydration products via their nucleating effect. Meanwhile, the W b value increases as the size of the PDs decreases. Specifically and the SPDs-C group displays the highest W b value of 17.18 %, representing a 16.47 % improvement compared to the blank group. This demonstrates that PDs with the smallest sizes showcase the greatest enhancement in the formation of cement hydration products.
(5)

Deriving the proportion of chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding in PDs-incorporated paste specimens is essential for elucidating the PDs-induced improvement mechanism of chloride binding. Hence, on the basis of the DTG analysis, the chemically bound chloride (
C b
Chem .
, primarily caused by the transformation from AFm phases to Fs through ion exchange [53]) is determined through Equation (3); While the physically adsorbed chloride (
C b
Phy .
, mainly attributed to the adsorption by C–S–H via electrostatic interaction or van der Waals force [53]) is quantified by Equation (4). The outcome of the calculation is illustrated in Fig. 10b. Undoubtedly, both
C b
Phy .
and
C b

values amplify after the incorporation of PDs in comparison to the blank group and gradually increase with the reduction of PDs sizes. Consequently, the SPDs-C group possesses the highest
C b

values at 34.33 mg/g and 19.73 mg/g, signifying an enhancement of 282 % and 35 % compared with the blank group separately. This indicates that cement pastes containing the smallest sizes of PDs exhibit the greatest chloride binding capacity because of the higher presence of C–S–H and Fs.

The microscopic morphology of diverse paste specimens after 28-day curing is presented via SEM imaging (Fig. 11 ). As for the blank group without PDs (Fig. 11a), The unhydrated particles measuring 6–8 μm display smooth surfaces. Meanwhile, the amorphous gel-like C–S–H is insufficient, exhibiting a disorganized arrangement and haphazardly sticking to unhydrated particles. This corroborates that the blank group presents inadequate cement hydration, thus resulting in a lack of C–S–H generation. After incorporating PDs (Fig. 11b–d), unhydrated particles undergo further dissolution, reducing to smaller sizes of 2–4 μm. At the same time, there are more C–S–H covering their surfaces. More significantly, the C–S–H content is progressively rising as the PDs sizes decrease. Especially in the SPDs-C group, unhydrated particles are almost negligible, superseded by a large quantity of C–S–H that is compactly distributed. This suggests that PDs facilitate the dissolution of clinker and the formation of more C–S–H; And the smaller-sized PDs bring about more pronounced hydration promotion of cement pastes. This is conducive to improving mechanical properties, as well as chloride physical adsorption of cement-based materials.

In reference to the samples following a 28-day submersion in a NaCl solution (Fig. 12 ), the unhydrated particles is scarcely detectable. Conversely, large hexagonal CH sheets that exceed 3 μm, as well as amorphous C–S–H and hexagonal-like flaky Fs crystals measuring 1–3 μm. As for the blank group (Fig. 12a), the CH sheets is sporadically adhered by a few of amorphous C–S–H and it is hard to observe the presence of Fs. As the dosage of PDs elevates (Fig. 11b–d), an increased amount of C–S–H is uniformly spread across the CH sheets; While the distribution of Fs crystals is increased and evenly distributed. Crucially, the quantity of C–S–H and Fs further increases as the sizes of PDs reduce. This is more noticeable in the SPDs-C group (Fig. 12d), further revealing that PDs with the smallest sizes facilitate the production of more C–S–H and their uniform distribution. This enhances the chloride physical adsorption and mechanical characteristics. Meanwhile, the smallest-sized PDs promote the generation of Fs crystals and boost their uniform distribution, consequently augmenting the chloride chemical binding. These findings are consistent with the XRD and DTG analyses.

To further comprehend the size effects of PDs on the mechanical capability of cement matrices, MIP is applied to analyze the pore structure of different paste specimens. As illustrated in Fig. 13 a, across all the specimens, the blank group manifests the largest cumulative pore volume. This detrimentally impacts the mechanical performances of the cement matrices. In the case of PDs-containing specimens, there is a gradual reduction in cumulative pore volume correlating with the decrease in PDs sizes. And the SPDs-C group with the smallest-sized PDs exhibits the minimum cumulative pore volume. This demonstrates that the addition of PDs dwindles the cumulative pore volume within cement matrices. It is probably ascribed to the PDs-facilitated generation and densification of hydration products, consequently refining the porosity in cement matrices. Furthermore, PDs with smaller sizes bring about a more refined pore volume of cement matrices, explaining the heightened efficacy of smaller-sized PDs in improving the mechanical properties of cement pastes (Fig. 6). Fig. 13b presents the pore size distribution of different specimens. Relative to the blank group, the pore volume of the PDs-containing specimens diminishes across the entire range of pore size, with the SPDs-C group invariably exhibiting the lowest pore volume across all the pore diameters. This further confirms that PDs with the smallest size contribute to the refinement of the pore structures within cement matrices since they more markedly enhance hydration promotion.

Based on the pore size and their corresponding influence, the pores in cement can be classified into four categories: harmless pores (under 20 nm), little harmful pores (20–50 nm), harmful pores (50–200 nm), and much harmful pores (over 200 nm) [54]. Fig. 14 depicts the total porosity, the porosity of each specific pore category, and the percentage of each pore type in various specimens. As demonstrated in Fig. 14a, the overall porosity of PDs-containing specimens is lower than that of the blank group (18.38 %), and PDs with smaller sizes lead to lower porosity of cement matrices. In particular, the SPDs-C group possesses the lowest total porosity (15.25 %), followed by the MPDs-C group (16.57 %), and the LPDs-C group (18.03 %). This provides strong evidence that the introduction of PDs drastically refines the porosity of cement matrices by facilitating the generation and densification of hydration products, and this enhancement effect is more noticeable with smaller PD sizes. More remarkably, as displayed in Fig. 14b, there is a noticeable increase in the ratio of harmless pores in PDs-containing specimens, along with a reduction in the percentage of harmful pores. Among them, the SPDs-C group has the highest percentage of harmless pores (33.38 %) and the lowest percentage of more harmful pores (12.41 %), increased by 12 % and decreased by 33 % than the blank group, respectively. This reaffirms that PDs-doped specimens share a finer pore structure, thus contributing to enhancing the mechanical properties of cement-based materials.

To summarize, the aforementioned results of phase composition and microstructure from diverse paste specimens corroborate that PDs with sizes ranging from 2.55 to 13.69 nm can enhance C–S–H production and improve its densification, thereby boosting the mechanical performance and chloride physical adsorption of cement composites. Concurrently, PDs are effective in boosting the generation of AFm phases and facilitating their transformation into Fs, thereby improving the chemical binding of chloride. Critically, the interconnection between PDs sizes and the phase composition/microstructure of paste specimens is elucidated. Namely, PDs with smaller sizes more vigorously boost the formation of hydration products, thus dramatically refining the pore structure of cement matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influencing mechanism of PDs sizes on the performance of cementitious materials
ce:section.ce:para: By integratively analyzing the characteristics of PDs with various sizes, the phase compositions and microstructures of different paste specimens, the mechanisms behind the size effects of PDs on the mechanics and chloride binding ability of cement pastes are reasonably disclosed.

The correlation between PDs dimensions and mechanical improvement of cement pastes is rationally presented (Fig. 15 ). Namely, PDs with smaller sizes boost the hydration process of cement to form a greater amount of C–S–H by their nucleating effect, and simultaneously facilitate C–S–H densely distributed within cement matrices to refine pore structure. This results in the elevated compressive strength of specimens. For one thing, on the basis of the reported nucleation theory [55], the nucleating effect of PDs can be explained by the lower nucleation energy of hydration products on their surface than that in the pore solution, resulting in preferential adherence of hydration products to the surface of PDs and promoting their crystallization process. This enables PDs to markedly enhance the hydration degree of cement. For another thing, the larger-sized PDs contain a reduced number of nanoparticles at an equivalent content, giving rise to diminished availability of nucleation sites for hydration products. Additionally, there is a negative correlation between the specific surface area and the particle sizes of nanomaterials [28]. The specific surface area of PDs increases, and the quantity of nucleation sites they provide correspondingly rises [56,57]. Therefore, the smaller-sized PDs can offer a greater number of nucleation sites, bringing about a more efficient enhancement of C-S-H production. Simultaneously, the porosity within cement matrices is significantly reduced owing to the further generation of hydration products promoted by smaller-sized PDs, thus effectively improving their mechanical properties. In detail, the largest-sized LPDs (13.69 nm) (Fig. 15a) exert an inferior nucleating effect, playing a limited role in promoting C–S–H formation. This results in insufficient C–S–H and further leads to high total porosity of the cement matrices. Hence, the LPDs-C group exhibits the lowest compressive strength among all the PDs-containing paste specimens. With the reduction of PDs sizes to 5.81 nm (i.e. MPDs) (Fig. 15b), the amount of C–S–H in the MPDs-C group showcases a noticeable elevation due to a stronger nucleating effect induced by MPDs. Meanwhile, the total porosity and the ratio of much harmful pores enormously decline, and the ratio of harmless pores increases. This leads to improved compressive strength in the MPDs-C group. Referring to the SPDs-C group containing SPDs with the smallest size of 2.55 nm (Fig. 15c), SPDs exert the most effective nucleating effect to generate more C–S–H. Then C–S–H tends to be densely distributed, further refining the porosity of cement matrices. Therefore, the SPDs-C group exhibits the most C–S–H content and the lowest total porosity, ultimately giving rise to the highest compressive strength. These results above are substantiated by compressive strength test, XRD, DTG, SEM, and MIP analyses. All in all, PDs exhibit a nucleating effect, boosting the generation of C–S–H and further refining the porosity of cement matrices, thus elevating the mechanical properties of cement pastes. Crucially, this improvement is directly proportional to the refinement of PDs sizes ranging from 2.55 to 13.69 nm.

The influence mechanism of PDs size effects on chloride binding within cement pastes is meticulously put forward. That is, smaller-sized PDs are capable of more effectively promoting the formation of C–S–H and Fs through their nucleating effect (discussed in Section 3.6.1), thereby substantially enhancing both the chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding capacities of cementitious materials. Specifically, as depicted in Fig. 16 a, the LPDs-C group owns the largest-sized PDs (13.69 nm), showcasing the minimum specific surface area, and possessing the least particle amount. As a result, the nucleation sites of LPDs are scarce, giving rise to insufficient contents of C–S–H and Fs. Therefore, the enhancement in physical adsorption and chemical binding of chloride is not substantial. As the particle size of PDs decreases, both the specific surface area and quantity of PDs progressively escalate. As for the MPDs-C group containing MPDs with a size of ca. 5.81 nm (Fig. 16b), the greater number of nucleation sites results in a higher hydration degree on cement pastes, thereby facilitating C–S–H and Fs production to further bound chloride ions. In terms of the SPDs-C group containing the smallest-sized SPDs measuring around 2.55 nm (Fig. 16c), the smallest SPDs provide the greatest number of nucleation sites, thus dramatically enhancing the hydration process of cement to further form C–S–H and Fs. Consequently, the SPDs-C group manifests the strongest chloride binding capacity. All the results are certified by chloride adsorption equilibrium test, XRD, DTG, and SEM measurement. Overall, PDs provide nucleation sites that facilitate cement hydration, leading to increased production of C–S–H and Fs, thus strengthening the chloride physical adsorption and chemical binding behaviors. Furthermore, a reduction in the sizes of PDs results in a greater number of nucleation sites to promote more generation of C–S–H and Fs, thus amplifying the chloride binding ability of cementitious materials.

In short, the size effects of PDs on mechanical performances and chloride binding ability are mainly determined by the nucleating effect exerted by PDs. Specifically, smaller-sized PDs result in more PD-induced nucleation sites, leading to a stronger enhancement effect on the performance of cementitious materials.

For the first time, this study thoroughly reveals the influence of PDs particle sizes on the mechanical capacity and the chloride binding capability of cement-based materials. In detail, three categories of PDs with various sizes are systematically prepared through the Schiff-based reaction only controlling the reaction temperature. Subsequently, the mechanical capacity and chloride binding behavior of diverse PDs-containing paste specimens are comprehensively determined. More importantly, the related mechanism is rationally unveiled. The primary findings are outlined as follows.
(1)
PDs with three distinct average sizes of 13.69, 5.81, and 2.55 nm (i.e., LPDs, MPDs, and SPDs) can be controllably fabricated by altering the reaction temperature of 25, 50, and 70 °C, respectively. As the reaction temperature elevates, the sizes of PDs gradually decrease. Moreover, these as-synthesized PDs possess identical surface groups and similar chemical compositions. More importantly, all the PDs demonstrate staggering long-term dispersion stability within the SCPs, providing a solid groundwork for their utilization in cementitious materials.
(2)
The correlation between the particle sizes of PDs and the mechanical performance/chloride binding property of PDs-containing paste specimens is investigated. Specifically, after 28-day curing, the LPDs-C group, MPDs-C group, and SPDs-C group show an escalation in mechanical properties by 3.8 %, 12.9 %, and 16.5 % separately in comparison to the blank group, and augments the chloride binding performance by 53 %, 117 %, and 129 % respectively after an additional exposure to NaCl solution for 28 d. Hence, the reduction of PDs sizes brings about more efficacious improvement of the mechanical characteristic and chloride binding ability of cement pastes.
(3)
According to the findings from XRD, DTG, SEM, and MIP tests, all the PDs-containing specimens substantially facilitate the formation and densification of hydration products (primarily C–S–H and Fs), and the pore structure of PDs-containing specimens is greatly refined. Crucially, PDs with smaller sizes result in more generation of hydration products and smaller total porosity of cement matrices.
(4)
The size effects of PDs on both mechanical properties and chloride binding performance of cement-based materials are rationally ascribed to the fact that smaller-sized PDs more effectively exert their nucleating effect to promote the production of hydration products. For mechanical properties, benefiting from the outstanding nucleating effect of smaller-sized PDs, a greater number of C–S–H are generated and form a dense distribution, thereby refining the porosity of cement matrices; For chloride binding capacity, the smaller-sized PDs can more remarkably facilitate the production of C–S–H and Fs through their superior nucleating effect, thereby dramatically amplifying the physical adsorption and chemical binding of chloride in cement pastes respectively.

This study thoroughly clarifies the size effects of PDs on the mechanical characteristics and chloride binding ability of cementitious materials, providing unparalleled insights to synthesize PDs suitable for improving their strength and chloride corrosion resistance. This would foster the advancement of nano-modified high-performance cement composites.

An-Nan Zhong: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Chuang He: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Shu-Yi Zheng: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Hong-Ru Guo: Visualization, Investigation. Wu-Jian Long: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
